title: Number Representation & Place Value
objective: Students will explore number representation & place value concepts including Place Value × 10 and ÷ 10, Reading and writing numbers (age 10+), Fractions on a number line (age 11+) and more.

steps.content: Have you ever thought about Place Value × 10 and ÷ 10? Let's find out together!
Recognise that in a multi-digit number, a digit in one place represents 10 times as much as in the place to its right and 1/10 of what it represents in the place to its left
Let me show you something cool. Explain that the 4 in 0.04 is 1/10 of the 4 in 0.4
Can you explain that in 4.56, the digit 5 is worth 5 tenths — ten times less than the 5 in 54, and ten times more than the 5 in 0.056?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Place Value × 10 and ÷ 10. Explain that the 4 in 0.04 is 1/10 of the 4 in 0.4 State that a digit moving one place left is ×10 and one place right is ÷10
Have you ever thought about Reading and writing numbers (age 10+)? Let's find out together!
Read, write, and compare decimals to thousandths using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form; compare using >, =, < based on place-value meaning
Can you read and write decimals like 3.047 or 0.625, say what each digit means (3 ones, 0 tenths, 4 hundredths, 7 thousandths), and put a set of decimals in order?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Reading and writing numbers (age 10+): Write 347.392 in expanded form: 3×100 + 4×10 + 7×1 + 3×(1/10) + 9×(1/100) + 2×(1/1000) Compare 0.372 and 0.38 using place-value reasoning
Today we're going to learn about Fractions on a number line (age 11+). Get ready for an adventure!
Order positive and negative integers, decimals, and fractions on a number line; use the symbols =, ≠, <, >, ≤, ≥ to compare values including negative numbers and mixed representations
Time to try this out: Order a mixed set of positive and negative integers, decimals, and fractions
If you is given a mixed list like 2.5, −3, ¾, 0, and −0.5, can they put them in order from least to greatest using a number line if needed?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Fractions on a number line (age 11+). Order a mixed set of positive and negative integers, decimals, and fractions Use the symbols =, ≠, <, >, ≤, ≥ correctly in mathematical statements
Today we're going to learn about Numbers to Ten Million. Get ready for an adventure!
Solve number and practical problems involving reading, writing, ordering, comparing, rounding, and negative numbers up to 10,000,000
Let's practice together! A country's population is 8,274,500 — round to the nearest million for a headline
If you reads that the distance to the Moon is about 384,400 km, can they round it to the nearest 10,000 km and say whether it's closer to 380,000 or 390,000?

Great job! Today we learned about Numbers to Ten Million. A country's population is 8,274,500 — round to the nearest million for a headline Order the depths of three ocean trenches given in metres including negative values
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Reading and writing numbers to 10,000,000!
Read, write, order, and compare numbers up to 10,000,000 and determine the value of each digit
Let me show you something cool. Write 4,302,561 in words
Can you read and write numbers up to 10 million — like 4,302,617 — and say the value of any digit in the number?
Great job! Today we learned about Reading and writing numbers to 10,000,000. Write 4,302,561 in words Order four seven-digit numbers from smallest to largest
Today we're going to learn about Reading Decimal Places. Get ready for an adventure!
Identify the value of each digit in numbers given to three decimal places (e.g. in 4.378, the 7 represents 7 hundredths)
Let me show you something cool. State the value of each digit in 2.635 (2 ones, 6 tenths, 3 hundredths, 5 thousandths)
In the number 4.378, can you tell you that the 7 represents 7 hundredths — not 7 tenths or 7 thousandths?

Great job! Today we learned about Reading Decimal Places. State the value of each digit in 2.635 (2 ones, 6 tenths, 3 hundredths, 5 thousandths) Explain the relationship between adjacent decimal places (each place is ten times smaller)
Today we're going to learn about Fractions on a number line. Get ready for an adventure!

Understand and use place value for decimals, measures, and integers of any size; extend the number system to include all positive and negative integers, decimals, and fractions on a single number line
Let me show you something cool. Identify the value of any digit in numbers of any size including decimals
Can you place a mix of numbers — like 3, −2, 0.5, −1.75, and ¼ — all on the same number line, in the right order?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Fractions on a number line: Identify the value of any digit in numbers of any size including decimals Place positive and negative integers, decimals, and fractions on a number line
Let's explore Decimal place value! Have you ever wondered about this?
Round decimals to any place using place-value understanding; round any whole number to a required degree of accuracy
Time to try this out: Round 3.4567 to 2 decimal places (3.46)
Can you round a decimal like 3.748 to one decimal place (3.7) or two decimal places (3.75) — and explain which digit they look at to decide?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Decimal place value: Round 3.4567 to 2 decimal places (3.46) Round 7,654,321 to the nearest 100,000 (7,700,000)
Today we're going to learn about Decimal place value (age 11+). Get ready for an adventure!
Round numbers and measures to an appropriate degree of accuracy including to a specified number of decimal places or significant figures
Let me show you something cool. Round numbers to a given number of decimal places
Can you round a measurement like 12.3746 km to 2 decimal places (12.37 km) or 3 significant figures (12.4 km) — and explain when each level of precision is appropriate?
Great job! Today we learned about Decimal place value (age 11+). Round numbers to a given number of decimal places Round numbers to a given number of significant figures
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Square and cube numbers!
Use integer powers and associated real roots (square, cube, and higher); recognise powers of 2, 3, 4, and 5; distinguish between exact representations of roots and their decimal approximations
Let me show you something cool. Calculate squares, cubes, and higher integer powers of whole numbers
Can you work out that √25 = 5 and ∛27 = 3 — knowing these are the inverse of squaring and cubing — and say whether √50 is closer to 7 or 8?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Square and cube numbers: Calculate squares, cubes, and higher integer powers of whole numbers Find square roots and cube roots of perfect squares and perfect cubes
Let's discover the amazing world of Measuring temperature!
Use negative numbers in context (temperature, finance, sea level); calculate intervals across zero
Let me show you something cool. The temperature falls from 3°C to −5°C — what is the drop? (8 degrees)
If a bank account goes from −£45 to +£120, can you work out the total change — counting through zero to get £165?
Great job! Today we learned about Measuring temperature. The temperature falls from 3°C to −5°C — what is the drop? (8 degrees) Calculate the difference between a bank balance of −£120 and £350
Have you ever thought about Patterns with Powers of Ten? Let's find out together!

Explain patterns in zeros when multiplying by powers of 10 and in decimal-point placement when multiplying/dividing by a power of 10; use whole-number exponents to denote powers of 10 (e.g. 10³ = 1000)
Let me show you something cool. Write 10⁴ = 10,000 and explain the exponent means four factors of 10
Can you explain why 10² = 100 and 10³ = 1,000, and why multiplying by 10 just adds a zero — connecting the pattern of zeros to place value?
Great job! Today we learned about Patterns with Powers of Ten. Write 10⁴ = 10,000 and explain the exponent means four factors of 10 Explain why 3.4 × 10² = 340 by describing the decimal shift
Have you ever thought about Numbers on a number line? Let's find out together!

Understand the absolute value of a rational number as its distance from zero on the number line; interpret absolute value as magnitude in real-world contexts; distinguish absolute value comparisons from ordering statements
Let's practice together! Define absolute value as distance from zero on a number line
Can you tell you that the absolute value of −7 is 7 — because it's 7 steps from zero on a number line regardless of direction — and use this idea to compare distances?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Numbers on a number line: Define absolute value as distance from zero on a number line Calculate the absolute value of positive and negative rational numbers
